figures.caption: Table 2: Test- and train-time OVSS results (mIoU%) on the nuScenes (NS) and SemanticKITTI (SK) validation sets. IGLOSS = IGLOSS(ScaLR+(DINOv2)), IGLOSSmix = IGLOSS(ScaLR+(DINOv2) OpenScene), OpenScene is distilled from OpenSeg. OV: ✗ = not OV at test time; ± = bias at training time with a predefined vocabulary or text prompts but functionally OV; (✓) = bias at training time from a captioner but still OV; ✓ = fully OV. 3D: ✓ = input is only 3D points; ✗ = input includes images. ens: ✗ = no ensembling; ✓ = VFM ensembling (at train or test time).
Table 7: Classwise semantic segmentation results (IoU%) of IGLOSS and its variants on nuScenes val set.
Fig. 1: IGLOSS vs typical 3D OVSS methods. Closed-set 3D OVSS methods
Fig. 6: Qualitative results of semantic segmentation from the validation sets of nuScenes and SemanticKITTI. The color code used to represent each class is provided in Tab. 13.
Table 6: Ensembling prototypes from various sources: ChatGPT-5 (Plus) [67], Gemini 2.5 (Pro) [27], Flux [46] and Google Image Web Search, tested on NS val set.

Fig. 5: Number of prototype images. (a) We use an increasing number of randomly generated ChatGPT images per prompt. Performance (on NS val set) plateaus at two. (b) Five random experiments with two images/prompt show a small variance.
Table 13: color codes used to represent each class of nuScenes or SemanticKITTI in Fig. 6 and Fig. 7.

Fig. 7: Qualitative comparison of semantic segmentation on the nuScenes validation set using our method and OpenScene [74]. The color code used to represent each class is provided in Tab. 13. Our method produces more faithful results by detecting other flat in the first row, segmenting trailer in the second row, and segmenting road and pedestrians in the third row.

Table 4: ScaLR+ ablation study. We study ScaLR+ distillation of DINOv2 in WaffleIron [77] (WI-48-256) on the mini-train and mini-val sets of nuScenes defined in [78, 86], varying: the drop-path proportion (Drop), the type of non linearity (Non lin.), the backbone head (Head), the image resolution (Resol.) and the number of epochs (Epochs). We use a 2-layer MLP. Hidden dimension is 2048 as in [30]. We measure the quality of features extracted before the head with linear probing (LP) and the impact on IGLOSS when classifying with nearest neighbor (NN) or logistic regression (LR).

Fig. 2: Examples of segment retrieval for arbitrary classes. As IGLOSS is OV, it can segment any class defined by a free text prompt. We present (points reprojected onto images) OV results for usually-ignored classes wheelchair, stroller, and crosswalk, which are not included in the 16 official nuScenes classes [5]. Crosswalks are not even identified in nuScenes and are just labeled as any driveable surface. Although crosswalk points are geometrically on the road plane, their features nonetheless identifies them specifically because the 2D-3D distillation takes lidar intensity into account [78].

Table 11: Our classwise text prompts used to generate prototype images for SemanticKITTI. Templates Tobj and Tdr-stuff are defined in Tab. 9. Only a few prompts are specific to SemanticKITTI; for other classes, the prompts are the same as in nuScenes for similar classes. In practice, we do not generate new images; we just reuse the same prototype images already generated for nuScenes.
Table 10: Our classwise text prompts used to generate prototype images for nuScenes. Templates Tobj, Tstuff, Tdr-obj and Tdr-stuff are defined in Tab. 9.
